content:
•

This is for hobby coding and on a modest laptop

I get it if you just want to stick to an OSS option, but I don't think either of those are show stoppers for clion either. I believe it's free to use for non-commercial use, and while the JB IDEs are reasonably heavy-weight, they're certainly still usable on a modest laptop, particularly if it's mainly for specific tasks like debugging. It might be worth a try if you haven't.

•

The shitty thing about relying on "free for non-commercial use" is that it doesn't take much to add some ads/sponsorships to a project to earn a little bit of money, or package a project up as an app which you sell for $1 on Google Play, or for someone to commission a small software thing, or any number of other seemingly innocent things which now means you're doing commercial development.

Use CLion if you want, but IMO only do that if you're ready to pay the real price (even if you happen to get away with the "free for commercial use" option right now). Because switching tools once you start doing something commercial and have years of muscle memory with CLion, is hard.

•

That's the truth. I used to get my (full toolbox) subscription reimbursed by my company. I moved on a while ago, but I've continued to pay the renewal since then (I moved on before they introduced the free non-commercial versions) because I like the tools so much and they do save me at least their subscription fees' worth of time. So very adjacent to what you describe. Also, they maintain IdeaVim, and I find it to be a really solid implementation of all the vim features I like.

(I routinely use pycharm and clion, and occasionally use datagrip and intellij. I've been using rustrover for the past few weeks too. If I didn't have them, I'd spend gobs of time fussing with my editor config. Source: I used to spend gobs of time fussing with my editor config, 10 years ago.)

•

Sure, but it makes it easy to try and see if it's a fit first. And at their individual pricing, a CLion sub costs less than a Netflix one anymore, and gives a perpetual license if you don't need upgrades...so if you make any money with it, or even just take your hobby seriously, seems like it would pretty easily justify itself.

Not trying to push anyone there if it's not what you want... but I have seen a lot of people dismiss jetbrains stuff initially, and then get hooked later when they actually try it. I still spend more time in tmux & vim than anything, but find it well worth it to keep the jb all products sub active so I can use it when it makes sense.

•

I recently used kdevelop just a little bit. Not enough to form a strong opinion about it, but it was decent to use on a 2018 ThinkPad, so it might be a fit depending on how modest you mean, exactly. And the debugger front-end was good for as much as I used it.

I've also got this saved in my notebook about fiddling with stm32. I haven't tried it personally, but it looks very promising for debugging native code, not just cross-debugging:

https://github.com/rohanrhu/gdb-frontend
